data.title: Silver Prices Will Surge To Triple Digits If Investors Panic
data.description:
It has been a while since my last article about silver. When I last wrote about the market for this precious metal in October, there was a BRICS summit where economic and political issues were discussed. Gold, as well as silver, surged to multi-year highs as there were speculations that the BRICS members would start shifting away from the dollar. As I wrote in my previous article about the US economy, President Trump was highly worried that the alliance would create a common currency, which would allow them to move away from the USD as the medium of exchange in international trade. Since that hype, both gold and silver prices have corrected somewhat only to rise again. Yet, they are still trading off their all-time highs reached in October last year. In this article, I will write about silver's potential to rise further.

Silver prices—what has happened?

The graph below shows the recent two years of silver’s price gains. As you can see, a lion’s share of these price increases was made in the first half of the year 2024. As I have also mentioned above, silver touched multi-year highs in October last year during the BRICS+ summit in Russia’s Kazan.

But what are the reasons for the most recent rally? After President Trump’s inauguration at the end of January this year, Trump’s administration started introducing tariffs against several countries, including China. Why did silver react then? Silver, just like gold, is highly sensitive to economic and political threats. This means that investors rush to buy these safe-heaven metals to protect their savings. This also happened this time, it seems to me.

Why is silver worth investing in?

After all, it is not gold. It is in much greater supply than the yellow precious metal. Moreover, in many countries there has been a gold standard. This means that a particular currency’s value was tied to gold. For example, in the US before Nixon’s USD reform, one ounce of gold was used to trade for $35. In other words, the price was fixed, and gold was convertible into dollars at that price. After Nixon’s reform, gold’s supply remained unchanged, while the U.S. Federal Reserve was allowed to print more money to finance the ballooning national budget’s deficits. The gold price, therefore, was not tied to the dollar’s value and started surging as the USD supply was rising.

But what do gold prices have to do with silver? After all, silver standards have never been very popular, unlike gold standards. However, in the eyes of investors, silver has been considered to be a “small brother” of gold. In other words, it is considered a cheaper and more accessible alternative to gold and also as a hedge against inflation because the money supply often rises at a much faster pace than silver reserves.

Apart from being a safe-haven metal, silver is also an industrial metal used in green energy—most importantly, solar batteries—jewelry, the production of semiconductors and other electronics. So, what are the key factors determining the silver demand and therefore its prices?

The key factors behind silver’s demand

As I have mentioned in my other articles about silver, there are several factors influencing the demand for the shiny gray metal, namely monetary policies, political events, and industrial demand. Industrial demand is mostly linked to factors like green energy, electronics goods like smartphones and laptops, and even artificial intelligence ('AI'). In one of my previous articles about silver, I wrote about the way silver is useful in AI technologies and why it could get very popular.

However, inflation, other macroeconomic indicators, political events and trends, and general uncertainty also affect investors’ appetite for silver, more than do industrial demand trends. Most of the demand for silver, therefore, is speculative. In other words, if there is market panic due to the Fed’s policy shift—in other words, more policy easing than previously expected—unforeseen political events or many professional investors rushing to buy silver, then private investors are likely to follow suit.

Is silver really that expensive?

As you can see from the graphs above, silver appears to be really expensive. However, let us see the longer history graph of silver prices, adjusted for inflation.

So, as you can see from the diagram above, the current price of over $30 per ounce is quite average, neither too high nor particularly low. There were high peaks in 2011 and 1980 accordingly. Adjusted for today’s money, their prices would have been almost $70 in 2011 and just over $140 in 1980.

As you can see even from the graph unadjusted for inflation above, silver prices are still off their peaks.

But how about the relationship between silver quantity and dollar liquidity? Well, it is quite direct. As I have mentioned above, even though silver was never directly tied to the USD the way gold was, many investors have rushed to buy silver coins, bars, or cutlery to compensate for the fiat currency’s depreciation. The most obvious situation when it happened was in 1980, when the Hunts speculated with the prices for the precious metal and smaller investors followed their example. It is well possible that prices might even touch $140 per ounce in today's money if large investors show a significant interest in the gray, shiny metal.

Silver used to serve as a medium of exchange in earlier centuries. It was used to make coins that then played the role of money.

So, how about the dollar-to-silver ratio nowadays? It is currently standing at $1,163 for one ounce of silver, according to the US Debt Clock website. True, the five-year average is even higher than that, currently standing at $1,561 for one ounce.

But bear in mind that in dollar terms, silver has been trading for just over $30 per ounce for a while, far below the dollar-to-silver ratio, which suggests its substantial undervaluation.

My take on the silver market

So, what does it all mean for the silver market? In simple terms, it means that silver is still undervalued and has further potential to rise in value. It is not stored by central bankers the way gold is. However, it is one of investors’ refuges in case of an economic or political crisis or softer monetary policies of central bankers. There might be a short-lived correction after a prolonged rally only for the prices to rise even further, in my opinion. The only question that remains is how to invest. In other words, some investors might prefer to buy stocks of silver miners. There is a risk, however, that investors might not be well-informed about a given company’s accounting fundamentals. Some investors, in turn, might buy futures, options, and ETFs. Unfortunately, there is much more paper silver than physical silver available. The paper-to-silver ratio is a whopping 378.39 to 1, according to the US National Debt Clock website. So, despite high storage costs, I am personally more in favor of investing in physical silver. I find it possible that silver prices may rise to $140 per ounce if there is substantial investor panic and key market players start buying silver.

data.title: Alibaba's Turnaround Is Finally Here
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Since my last Alibaba (NYSE:BABA) coverage, the stock has yielded a +38.6% return against the market’s negative 1.4%. After a long time, Alibaba’s turnaround is in full swing. With AI-driven cloud revenue surging triple digits for six straight quarters, Taobao and Tmall thriving, and international e-commerce up 36% YoY, the company is regaining dominance. Alibaba may be overbought in the short term, but healthy pullbacks provide strategic entry points for long-term investors as its AI, cloud, and e-commerce growth fuel sustained upside.

Q1-25 Target: $170 Base And $198 Bullish Amid Overbought Signals

Starting with Alibaba stock price targets for Q1-25, the average price target of $170 is in line with the 1.618 Fibonacci extension. The price target is based on the current market conditions and projected trend in stock performance. The optimistic target of $198 is aligned with the 2.118 Fibonacci extension that anticipates a bullish rally. Here, the price target indicates strong growth potential should market conditions favor Alibaba’s business expansion. However, the pessimistic target of $108 is based on the 0.382 Fibonacci retracement. It considers the potential for a pullback under a bearish trend possibly influenced by macro factors (like the US-China trade war) or company-specific issues (like AI pricing pressure).

However, the RSI value of 71.2 signals an overbought condition. It is suggesting that the stock is nearing its short-term peak. Since RSI is above 70, there’s a higher risk of a price correction. Why? The stock may have become too expensive relative to its recent performance. The absence of divergences suggests that while the stock is holding strength, it is not signaling a resumption or reversal pattern. The downward trend of the RSI line points to the potential weakness or at least a cooling off in upward momentum.

Finally, the volume price trend (VPT) is bullish. The VPT line is trending upwards at 357M and surpassing its 265M moving average. This indicates strong buying activity and suggests the potential for continued upward momentum in the stock price. Additionally, the seasonality factor marks a 55% probability of a positive return in February, based on historical trends over the past decade.

AI Revenue Maintains Triple-Digit Gains, E-Commerce Expands 36% Internationally

Alibaba’s overall revenue (excluding consolidated subsidiaries) jumped by 11% YoY. This growth was particularly based on the cloud computing segment. The segment had a 13% YoY increase in revenue. AI-related product revenue has maintained triple-digit YoY growth for six quarters back to back. With that, the company is capitalizing on AI advancements.

Moreover, e-commerce remains a business with Taobao and Tmall, which had a 9% increase in customer management revenue (CMR) based on improved monetization and higher online gross merchandise volume (GMV). The company’s international e-commerce revenue from retail operations grew by 36% YoY to RMB 31.6B and the wholesale segment increased by 18% to RMB 6.2B. In short, with these figures, Alibaba holds a strong lead in both domestic and global markets.

Further, Alibaba’s cloud business continues to gain momentum, with an acceleration in top-line growth to 13%. The company’s AI strategy has played a significant role in this expansion, as AI-related product revenue has consistently hit triple-digit YoY growth. The launch of Qwen 2.5 Max further solidifies Alibaba’s tech lead. Specifically, more than 90K derivative models have been developed globally and over 290K companies and developers have accessed Qwen APIs through Alibaba Cloud’s Bilian platform. These numbers point to the widespread adoption of Alibaba’s AI-based cloud services. This is a core element ensuring continued top-line growth in this segment.

Back to e-commerce, Alibaba’s domestic e-commerce businesses (Taobao and Tmall) have maintained strong growth in user engagement. The number of 88VIP members grew to 49M with double-digit growth. This loyalty program led to boosted customer retention and spending. Alibaba’s continuous investment in new user acquisition and platform enhancements has led to a jump in new consumers and orders. Internationally, AIDC’s cross-border business performance continues to solidify as revenue grew by 32% to RMB 37.8B in Q3. The segment’s investments in overseas shopping festivals and expansion into European and Gulf markets have led to user growth and improved unit economics.

Moreover, the company divested non-core assets, selling its interest in Senna for up to $1.6B and InTime for $1B. These strategic exits streamline operations and boost liquidity. The company also executed share buybacks totaling $1.3B this quarter, leading to a 0.6% net reduction in share count.

Over the last nine months, Alibaba has repurchased ~$10B in shares. This led to a 5% reduction in outstanding shares. The company raised $5B through a dual currency bond issuance, leveraging favorable RMB-denominated notes to lower financing costs. With a strong net cash position of RMB 378.5B ($51.9B), Alibaba has solid liquidity to fund investments (particularly in AI and cloud infrastructure).

Overall, these elements support a progressive topline. Now, considering the PS ratio along with the top-line growth, the stock is highly undervalued relative to its long-term average.

Alibaba’s $31B AI Bet: Growth Catalyst or Profitability Trap?

Alibaba’s aggressive investment in cloud and AI infrastructure poses a risk to its short-to-medium-term profitability. The company’s CapEx for Q3-25 was boosted ~2X to $31B and it plans to invest more in the next three years than it has in the past decade. This level of investment is massive and raises concerns about the company’s capability to maintain profitability amid scaling its infrastructure. These investments may lead to higher depreciation costs and will hit hard on margins. This is problematic given Alibaba’s cloud margins are already significantly lower than those of peers like AWS and Microsoft Azure.

Here, the pricing pressures are intense and margins are thinner against international markets. As Alibaba scales its AI and cloud infrastructure, the depreciation costs associated with these investments could further compress margins, making it hard to attain the required profitability in the near term and hitting the stock’s valuation hard. Moreover, the company’s vitality in maintaining high levels of CapEx over the next three years suggests that profitability may remain subdued for an extended period. While management expects these investments to derive long-term growth, the immediate financial impact could deter valuation.

Finally, Alibaba’s growth strategy is heavily reliant on its cloud and AI businesses. While the company has seen triple-digit growth in AI-related revenues for six consecutive quarters, the overall contribution of AI to total revenue remains relatively small. The company’s AI strategy is centered around pushing the limits in developing proprietary models, but the monetization pathways for these models are still unclear. For instance, Eddie Wu mentioned that 95% of the output tokens from AI models will be generated and distributed through the cloud. Here, the revenue potential from these tokens is not well defined. The AI market in China is becoming increasingly homogeneous, with narrowing differentiation between models from different vendors. This lack of differentiation can lead to price wars that can further erode margins and hit valuation.

Takeaway

Alibaba’s long-awaited turnaround is in motion, delivering a +38.6% return as AI and e-commerce fuel top-line growth. Cloud revenue is surging triple digits, international expansion is thriving, and aggressive buybacks are boosting shareholder value. However, a massive $31B AI investment threatens near-term profitability, with pricing pressures and margin risks looming. While the Q1-25 target of $170 ($198 bullish) signals strong momentum, RSI overbought conditions suggest a possible short-term pullback. Alibaba remains undervalued, but investors must weigh its AI ambitions against profitability risks in the coming quarters.

data.title: 3 Predictions For March
data.description:
We are winding down the trading week and the month of February today. The month saw a bit of a rollercoaster ride for investors late in the month with the NASDAQ falling nearly 2.8% on Thursday, February 27th. The NASDAQ has also had its fastest five percent drop since the Covid pandemic it should be noted. So, what might lie ahead for investors in the coming month that opens up trading on Monday?

Here are my three predictions for March.

Volatility Increases:

The S&P VIX Index (VIX) moved very briefly over the 20 level four times during February after not doing so once in January to commence 2025. I expect volatility to continue to rise in March and for the VIX to cross 25 at least once during the month.

There are several potential events that could trigger that additional volatility in March. First, the February CPI and PPI could continue to show the 'last mile' on the inflation front is getting somewhat intractable after both readings came above expectations in February. This could easily take any potential cut to the Fed Funds rate off the table at least through the first half of 2025.

Investors could see more signs of a slowing economy during the month. In February, the BLS jobs report showed only 143,000 positions created in January, 25,000 below expectations. Q4 GDP was revised down to 2.3% from its initial reading of 2.6% and significantly below the 3.1% growth of Q3. Consumer Sentiment also cratered in February. Walmart Inc. (WMT) offered up a rather tepid view of the consumer as we head into 2025 even after besting expectations with its fourth quarter numbers.

The budget process could hit an impasse, and the chances of a government shutdown could increase dramatically in the months ahead. Both the Senate and the House (barely) passed blueprints for the upcoming budget. However, as a recent article noted, the two versions differ substantially. With the House Republicans enjoying a razor-thin majority, the chances of several failed attempts at 'reconciliation', and worries about at least a temporary government shutdown are likely to increase in the coming weeks and months.

However, the most likely cause of disruption to the markets in March will be around trade policies, specifically tariffs. These have already caused spikes in volatility in equities in February. With 25% tariffs on most imported products from Mexico and Canada due to take effect next week after a month's delay, investors could get another taste of 'tariff turmoil' early in March. 'Reciprocal Tariffs' also will come to the forefront in March along with additional tariffs on China and new ones on Europe. It is hard to see how this topic doesn't cause considerable and additional angst in the markets during the coming month.

Tech Continues To Fall:

One of the reasons behind the recent pullback in tech stocks is that no sector in the market has a greater exposure to overseas sales. We saw this dynamic in action on Thursday as NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) fell some 8.5% in trading despite posting better-than-expected Q4 numbers and providing solid overall guidance for FY2025. The culprit was the company's CEO stating sales in China are half of what they were before the U.S. implemented export controls and other potential tariff impacts.

Given its large exposure to China, Apple Inc. (AAPL) is just one of many large tech companies that could see further turmoil in the market in March as tariff wars escalate. One of many reasons, the tech giant from Cupertino announced this week that it plans to invest more than $500 billion in the United States over the next four years.

Besides new tariffs and increasing worries that the economy is slowing, investors in the technology sector should be concerned about valuation levels after this part of the market was the key driver of stock market gains in 2023 and 2024. The aforementioned Apple trades at nearly 40 times trailing earnings and is at the top of its historical price to sales ratio valuation range as well. Even after falling by a third from its highs earlier this month, the shares, or AI-driven concern Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) are trading for north of 50 times FY2025E revenues. Given this, it seems likely tech will continue to fall in March.

Bitcoin Selloffs Further:

One of the major casualties as the market has gone into 'risk off' mode in recent trading sessions has been Bitcoin. The cryptocurrency has lost just over 20% of its value from its peak earlier this month. Bitcoin is in danger of giving up all of its post-election rallies at this point and crossed under its 200-day moving average this week. Favorable regulatory moves for cryptocurrency look like they are going to put off until the second half of 2025 at least and there has been a large recent outflow from Bitcoin ETFs.

The price of gold, on the other hand, has held up much better with the yellow metal off less than five percent from its peak. I would not fight the current momentum, and Bitcoin seems likely to fall further in March.

Given this view as we head into March, I remain cautious about the overall market. About a third of my portfolio is in short-term treasuries. Most of the rest is within covered call holdings on the relatively few stocks I am finding that still sport reasonable valuations. These include names like ACADIA Pharmaceuticals Inc. (ACAD), Lantheus Holdings, Inc. (LNTH), Collegium Pharmaceutical, Inc. (COLL) and ANI Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (ANIP), all of which have held up well this week as all three companies reported solid fourth quarter results in recent days.

data.title: American Express: A Better Entry Point Is Coming
data.description:
Credit card company American Express (NYSE:AXP) (NEOE:AXP:CA) is a solid blue-chip stock that has given investors a solid total return of 143% over the past five years. The company has a competitive advantage through its closed-loop network, upscale brand image – attracting wealthy clients – and exclusive rewards, allowing it to charge higher fees while enjoying strong customer loyalty and high spending per customer.

Financially speaking, the company has a great track record of growth and profitability. The numbers back the story.

However, while AXP stock should do well in the long term for investors buying at the current price, I think a better entry point will present itself at some point. The stock's valuation is a little elevated compared to historical norms, and I would prefer a larger drop before buying.

AXP stock gets a Hold rating from me.

Going Over American Express's Moat

AXP stock has returned a total of 25,000% since its IPO in 1972. Let's go over the company's moat and what makes it so strong.

As stated above, American Express operates a closed-loop payment network. For comparison, Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA), which are also great companies, operate an open-loop payment network. Essentially, American Express controls the whole process, as it issues the credit card (like a bank) and controls the payment processing. This allows Amex to collect interchange fees, interest income when people don't pay on time and annual card fees. Plus, it has full control over its fee structure.

It's a bit different from Visa and Mastercard, which basically act like a toll booth on transactions but rely on banks for things like card issuances.

The company also has a strong brand identity that attracts wealthy customers. For example, an article from three years ago reported that the average Amex Platinum cardholder had a net worth of $4.3 million and a household income of $474,000. Even more impressive, the average Amex Centurion cardholder (the "black card") had an average net worth of $11.4 million and a household income of $1.8 million.

Obviously, these are the higher-end Amex cards, but the point still stands that Amex has attracted many affluent clients, and these clients spend more money. Additionally, not only is it a status symbol to have a high-end Amex card, which is already enough to attract people to it, but customers like the perks that come with them, such as airport lounge access.

Amex Can Get Away With Charging Higher Fees

Amex charges more fees per transaction than Visa and Mastercard. Why? Because the company can. Here's how much each firm charges, according to bankrate.com:

Visa and Mastercard tend to charge credit card processing fees between 1.15 percent + $0.05 and 2.5 percent +$0.10 per transaction. American Express charges 1.43 percent + $0.10 to 3.30 percent + $0.10 per transaction. That means if a business sells $10,000 in credit card transactions per week, they could pay around $115 to $250 in Visa and Mastercard fees versus around $143 to $330 in Amex fees.

At first, it may not make much sense for merchants to accept Amex if they will pay more in fees. However, Amex can get away with it because of its wealthy customer base. Sure, the merchant will pay more in fees, but the merchant also knows that a wealthier customer may be willing to spend more than the average customer, increasing the amount of money earned overall.

At the moment, 80% of merchants accept Amex across the company's top 12 international countries. That's up eight percentage points compared to three years ago. Quote from AXP's Q4 earnings call:

We added millions of new merchant locations globally in 2024 and we reached an average of 80% coverage across our top 12 international countries with coverage in travel and entertainment categories well above 80%. That's an increase of eight percentage points from three years ago.

AXP's Financials Are Solid And Prove Its Strength

Since American Express is a financial company, it's useful to look at its return on equity. And that's where the stock shines. American Express's TTM ROE is 34.3% (much higher than the sector median of 10.45%), and its average from 2020 to 2024 is 29.1%, although that includes a sharp drop because of COVID-19. A more normalized ROE is over 30%. Also, the fact that the ROE has been relatively steady over the years highlights the strength of the business.

Notably, the company earns an A+ profitability grade on Seeking Alpha.

Its growth has been solid as well and may actually accelerate from here if management's long-term aspirations come to fruition. Over the past five years, its revenue has grown at a CAGR of 8.73%, while its 10-year CAGR is 6.62%. Meanwhile, the company's long-term aspiration is for revenue growth of 10%+, and 2025 revenue growth is expected at 8-10%.

EPS has grown nicely as well, at a CAGR of 9.7% over the past 10 years, with growth of 12-16% expected this year and a long-term aspiration of mid-teens growth.

The EPS growth rate should stay higher than revenue over the long term due to the accretive nature of buybacks, which management does a good amount of. The company's share count fell by 3% year-over-year in Q4 and has been falling for a while, boosting per-share results.

I'm Just Waiting For The Valuation To Fall Further

AXP's non-GAAP P/E ratio is around 21x right now, based on its TTM non-GAAP EPS of $14.01 and a share price of $294.20. That may not sound too expensive due to the growth potential, and you're right to think so, but it's expensive by AXP's standards. AXP stock doesn't tend to trade at high multiples in the first place. That's likely because its business has bank-like characteristics, and banks generally trade at low multiples.

Its five-year average, according to Seeking Alpha, is 19.64x. And its longer-term average, going all the way to 2006, is 17.5x.

Again, I'm not saying that AXP stock will never go up from here, but there's a good chance that its P/E ratio can contract eventually, hurting the return potential in the short term.

We can't ignore that the stock is no stranger to large dips and is just starting to pull back after a blistering rally starting in October 2023. I just don't expect the stock to reward shareholders in the short term in the same way it did recently.

The Takeaway

American Express is a high-quality company with a moat that can be quantified just by looking at its profitability and growth trends. Its closed-loop network, premium brand image, and customer loyalty give it pricing power. The company clearly has the ability to create long-term value for shareholders, and I don't think that will change anytime soon, especially with management aiming for 10%+ revenue growth and mid-teens EPS growth over the long term.

That being said, its valuation is a little bit elevated compared to historical norms, and the chart is also extended, following a huge rally starting in October 2023. AXP stock is still good for the long term, but a better entry point could come in the future, particularly if its P/E ratio falls to or below its long-term average.

A 10-20% dip would make me interested in the stock, but for now, it gets a Hold rating from me.

data.title: 68 Graham All-Star Value Dogs (GASV): Of 18 "Safer", Buy Any Of 14 Ideal February Choices
data.description:
Foreword

Definition excerpts below from YCharts:

About Large Cap Value

“A Value ranking for large cap stocks from YCharts puts together complementary strategies found during their stock research. The value ranking looks at the price of a stock relative to a number of measurements that determine intrinsic firm value. Only the largest 10% of companies based on market cap are shown in this portfolio.

What it is: The Value Score is a composite score. It tells you how much you are getting in terms of profits, cash flows, assets, sales, etc. for the stock price that you pay. It is a relative measurement, so it says nothing about the overall level of the market. Rather, it answers the question: "Given the current market level, which stocks give you the most current value for your dollar of investment?" Tens are the most value, and 1s are the least.

How to use it: Use it to find companies that are selling at a low price relative to their assets and profits.

Limitations of the Value Score: Watch out for companies with a lot of uncertainty or bad prospects for the future (e.g., Pharma companies with expiring patents, industries on the decline, etc.).

About the Ben Graham Formula

The Ben Graham Formula strategy contains ultra-stable stocks that will infrequently lose money if held over a long period of time. It was developed based on a screen in Graham's book, "The Intelligent Investor." For those who have the book, it is the "Defensive Investor" screen. It selects stocks that are large in terms of sales and total assets, have a strong track record of earnings and dividend payments, have a reasonable current ratio and level of long term debt, and have a low valuation given by PE Ratios and Price to Book Value ratios.”

-YCharts

While over half of this collection of GASV is too pricey, or reveal somewhat skinny or no forward looking dividends, fourteen of the eighteen “safer” lowest-priced Dogs of the GASV are ready to buy. February finds HSBC Holdings (HSBC), KS KKR Capital (FSK), Altria Group (MO), EnergyTransfer (ET), DHT Holdings (DHT),Verizon (VZ), First Interstate (FIBK), Bank of Nova Scotia (BNS), Columbia Banking (COLB), Suncor Energy (SU), Barclays (BCS), Banco Santander (SAN), Kronos Worldwide (KRO), on the buy list.

[See a summary of top ten fair-priced February GASV Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and all eighteen safe buys at the bottom of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 39.75% To 82.26% Net Gains From Top-Ten GASV Dogs By February 2026

Five of ten top GASV (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this February, 2025 yield-based forecast for them, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 50% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-26 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to February, 2026 were:

Pangaea Logistics (PANL) was projected to net $842.55 based on the median of target price estimates from 3 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 66% under the market as a whole.

International Seaways (INSW) was projected to net $803.91, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 8 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 87% under the market as a whole.

Guess? (GES) was projected to net $682.31, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 4 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 89% greater than the market as a whole.

Dorian LPG (LPG) was projected to net $633.06 based on the median of target prices estimated by 3 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 2% under the market as a whole.

TORM PLC (TRMD) was projected to net $593.17 based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 77% less than the market as a whole.

Petrobras (PBR) was projected to net $521.61, based on the median of target price estimates from 15 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 41% greater than the market as a whole.

Jiayin Group (JFIN) was projected to net $440.89, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this 11% under the market as a whole.

Vale SA (VALE) was projected to net $436.22, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 23 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 9% under the market as a whole.

Park Hotels (PK) was projected to net $423.20, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 14 analysts, less broker fees.The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 100% over the market as a whole.

The RMR Group (RMR) was projected to net $397.53 based on the median of target price estimates from 3 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 24% greater than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 57.75% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten GASV stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 24% greater than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

Top 50 February, 2025 GASV By Analyst Target Gains

Top 50 February, 2025 GASV By Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top February GASV Stocks By Yield Ranged 11.96% To 38.9% Per YCharts

Top ten GASV occupied five of eleven Morningstar sectors.

Five energy representatives placed first, second, and fifth, to seventh, Petrobas, [1], TORM [2], International Seaways [5], Dorian [6], and Flex LNG (FLNG) [7].

One materials stock placed third, Vale [3] and a lone real estate member placed fourth, Site Centers (SITC) [4]. A discretionary sector dog took the eighth position, Ford Motor (F) [8].

Finally, two financials stalwarts, placed ninth, and tenth, HSBC [9], and FS KKR [10], to complete the February Dogs of the GASV by yield.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The graph above shows relative strengths of the top ten February GASV by dividend and price as of market close 2/25/2025.

This month none of the top-ten GASV by yield show less than ideal status because the prices of all ten are less than the projected annual dividends from $1k invested in each. Yes, all ten are IDEAL.

A dividend dogcatcher priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share price. As mentioned above, that condition was reached by forty-nine of the highest yield GASV. The 68 January GASV Cost Per $1 of dividend ranged from $2.57 for Petrobras, to $155.55 for Itau Unibanco.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Twenty-one of Sixty-eight GASV Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Twenty-one GASV so infected are considered unsafe to buy.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, uncertainty, and the toll of trading tariffs.

No Price Drops or Dividend Increases Are Needed; All Ten Top Yield GASV Are ”Fair-Priced” For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, all the top ten are already ideally priced. The results from the bottom chart show that at the tipping point between fair and unfair, the top ten GASV have margins ranging from $10.44 to $60.11 or 16.1% to 85.72% before they break into the unfair territory.

These companies have no need to raise their dividends. Mr. Market is very effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts. Watch and buy after your targeted stock price moves into a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 23.61% To 72.54% Top Ten GASV Upsides To February, 2025; (32) Three -8.16% to -15.21% Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 4.57% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 GASV As of February 25, 2026

Ten-top GASVs were culled by yield for this monthly-update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten GASV selected 2/25/25 by yield, represented five of eleven sectors. Communication Services, Consumer Defensive, Health Care, Industrials, Technology, and Utilities, all missed the top-ten cut.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield Dividend GASV (34) To Deliver 42.25% Vs. (35) 40.41% Net Gains by All Ten Come February 20, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten GASV by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 4.57% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The eighth lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, INSW, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 80.29%.

The five lowest-priced top-yield GASV dogs for February were:

Ford; Vale; Petrobras; Site Centers; TORM, with prices ranging from $9.42 to $18.90.

Five higher-priced GASV top-yield dogs as of February 25 were: Dorian LPG; Flex LNG; FS KKR; International Seaways; HSBC, with prices ranging from $21.18 to $57.77.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the post-election euphoria in the market dies down and the feckless Fed-fueled slide and dithering presidential tariff pronouncements gain momentum into 2025, with the exception of the few tech stocks, look for more GASV to become Fair-priced… slow and sure, or suddenly.

Afterword

Buy 14 Ideal of 18 ‘Safer' February GASV Dogs

Late-February 2025, 18 of the 68 GASV were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. YCharts 2/25/25 data showed 14 were fair-priced.

How GASV 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Results Produced “Break-Even Price” Results For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that ten dividends from $1K invested in our top ten 'safer' dividend Aristocrats conform to the dogcatcher ideal of being equal to (or greater than) the price of a single share. Those ten ideal dogs were HSBC, FS KKR, Altria; Energy Transfer, DHT, Verizon; First Interstate, Bank of Nova Scotia, Columbia Banking, and Trust Financial.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock; a so-called, “break-even price.”

All ten share prices of these “safer” survivors are below or the dividend payouts from $1k invested. The top chart reveals that the ten show ranges of $4.63 to $29.50 or 0.99% to 56.78% in price increases to equal the dividend payout from $1k invested.

These GASV leaders fetch dividends about as “good” as they can get.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher GASV updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" and “Ideal” GASV dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice. Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article except as noted are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.

Graphs and charts were compiled by Rydlun & Co., LLC from data derived from Indexarb; YCharts; Yahoo Finance - Stock Market Live, Quotes, Business & Finance News; analyst mean target price by YCharts. Open source dog art from Dividend Dog Catcher

data.title: Repeated Disappointments On Growth And Margins Leaves Conmed Even Deeper In The Doghouse
data.description:
It is arguably unfair to say that nothing is going right for CONMED Corporation (NYSE:CNMD) (“Conmed”) these days, but there’s certainly not enough going right to get the stock working right now. Management’s prior revenue growth targets have proven much too ambitious and operational issues that I would argue should have been nixed some time ago continue to weigh on margins, and that’s without the potentially severe impact of tariffs on the business.

Conmed shares are down another 10% or so since my last update, underperforming the broader medical device space by close to 25%. While these shares do still look meaningfully undervalued, Conmed is looking at 2025 where a lot of effort will be expended without necessarily showing up in the final results. What’s more, the Street remains concerned with operational challenges to key growth products like AirSeal (from Intuitive Surgical, Inc.'s (ISRG) ongoing launch of its newest robot platform), and the reality is that mid-single-digit revenue growth and no margin leverage is a toxic mix for a small-cap med-tech stock.

I still think there’s value here, and patient investors may want to wait it out, but I can’t really say this is a great idea for new investment now.

A Good Quarter (Sort Of), But It Doesn’t Matter All That Much

I’ll discuss Conmed’s disappointing guidance (and potential downside risk to that guidance) in a moment, but a review of the fourth quarter is in order. While reported results were better than the Street expected, particularly at the operating income line, that should be viewed in the context that estimates came down throughout 2024 and the final results were weaker than what analysts had been expecting at the time of my last article.

Revenue rose 6% in organic terms, with Orthopedic Surgery revenue up more than 2% (and up more than 5% in the U.S.) and General Surgery up almost 9% (and up more than 7% in the U.S.). By product type, revenue from single-use products rose almost 10% (to almost 86% of the total), while capital equipment sales dropped more than 12%.

Adjusted gross margin rose 120bp (and 110bp sequentially) to 57.6%. Adjusted operating income rose 25%, with the margin up almost three points to 18.6%

Ortho sales continued to be limited by ongoing supply issues, and while AirSeal sales were up double-digits for the full year, Intuitive Surgical placements are going to pick up next year, creating some more near-term challenges (though Conmed has seen a lot of surgeons return to the product once they fulfill their procedural commitments).

Guidance Was The Falling Star Of The Show

Conmed management was clearly too aggressive with its expectations for 2024, and the backpedaling through the year certainly didn’t help sentiment, even if unexpected events like a hurricane impacting a Florida manufacturing facility are more or less forgivable. More to the point, the fact that supply issues continue to be an issue doesn’t reflect particularly well on the company.

With third quarter earnings the company’s now-former CEO announced his retirement and the company announced that the COO, Patrick Beyer, will take over, Since then guidance has come down further, and this could arguably be seen as the incoming CEO’s attempt to “clear the decks”, get all the bad news out there, and start more or less from a fresh page.

In any case, guidance for 2025 revenue was about 3% below already-lowered expectations and 4% to 6% revenue growth is well below the high single-digit to low double-digit growth that analysts were expecting from the business a year ago.

On top of that, management announced that they expected no margin expansion in 2025, as the company will be reinvesting in its supply efforts and working down inventories. On the supply side, the company ultimately realized it was too dependent upon single-source suppliers and now needs to remedy that issue. On the inventory side, the company simply has too much inventory built up in certain SKUs and is going to burn off that inventory, which will hurt manufacturing leverage in the near term.

That guidance was rough enough as it was (with a new EPS target range of $4.25 to $4.40 versus the Street’s prior $4.74), but it could get worse. Not only might further expansion of the Intuitive Surgical Da Vinci 5 rollout have a bigger-than-expected impact on AirSeal (one of Conmed’s best growth products), but tariffs could take a big bite out of earnings.

About a third of the company’s manufacturing takes place in a maquiladora in Mexico. If the threatened 25% tariff goes into effect, and there are no exemptions carved out for maquiladoras or consideration of value-added in Mexico, as much as 25% of company EPS could be at risk. Likewise, the expiration of an exemption to tariffs on products from China would add a small incremental negative effect on top of that.

There are still plenty of unknowns here. If the new tariff were to only apply to the value-add, it would reduce the impact by more than 80%, but I think that’s likely an optimistic assumption. It is also uncertain as to what the company may be able to do to minimize exposure – products manufactured in Mexico are shipped to a facility in Georgia for sterilization, and my understanding is that products meant for international markets could be shipped elsewhere, reducing the tariff impact.

The Outlook

It’s not all doom and gloom here. The company still has an overall attractive mix of businesses, including good exposure to above-average growth markets like foot/ankle, sports medicine, and smoke evacuation (where state-level mandates continue to expand the market). Moreover, AirSeal is far from dead, as surgeons who like the device continue to find ways to use it, and BioBrace bio-inductive implant could become a multi-hundred million dollar a year product for the company.

In the short term, though, there are obviously significant challenges that management must resolve. I’ve cut my 2025 revenue number by more than 6%, and with a lower expected growth rate over the next few years, my 2028 number is now almost 9% lower. I’m now looking for 3-5 year revenue growth in the 5% range and longer-term growth of a little more than 5%.

On the margin side, my expectations for FY’26 EBITDA margin have gone from around 23.5% to about 21% and a mid-20s percent EBITDA margin may not be possible within the next five years (though I would rather not underestimate potential leverage as the company exits this restructuring process and as products like BioBrace enter the mix). Likewise, my operating margin expectation for FY’25 is about 250bp lower now, and I’m no longer expecting double-digit ROCE within the next three years. All of this could get even worse if Conmed has to absorb the full impact of the threatened 25% tariff on goods imported from Mexico.

The good news, if you can call it that, is that the valuation was so undemanding before that there’s still a value argument to be made for the stock. Between discounted cash flow and margin/growth-driven EV/revenue, I can argue for a fair value of $79 to $81 against a current price in the low-$60s. I should note too that I’m using a lower EV/revenue multiple than what my multiple would normally support (2x versus 3x), as Conmed now fits into that slower-growing, weak margin leverage bucket where smaller stocks often languish with lower multiples.

The Bottom Line

Even with the full worst-case impact of tariffs, Conmed shares would still be modestly undervalued on discounted cash flow, so I can understand value-driven investors thinking they’ll just hunker down and ride out this rough patch.

I don’t necessarily disagree with that, but I’ve seen too many examples of slower-growing smaller med-techs turning into frustrating value traps to recommend that. I do still see multiple positive drivers here (in addition to valuation), but I think it may be best to take a step back for now, see what happens with tariffs, and see if management can drive some upside to revenue growth before getting more positive again.

data.title: BCD: A Longer Dated Strategy That Outperforms On The Commodities Market
data.description:
I generally avoid middle-ground solutions, especially with ETFs. However, I have to admit I'm increasingly appreciating a solution like Abrdn Bloomberg All Commodity Longer Dated Strategy K-1 Free ETF (NYSEARCA:BCD), even though it represents a soft compromise between short-term monetary funds and the commodities markets. In the last month, I've already expressed my interest in this approach while analyzing an alternative to BCD: PDBC. I must say that the current economic situation and its shifting prospects have once again directed my focus toward these types of funds, especially those that utilize a longer-dated strategy.

Longer-Dated Strategy vs. Standard Rolling Strategy ETF: An Important Insight to Keep in Mind

I was looking for an ETF in the commodities market with specific characteristics:

  • K-1 free.
  • Longer-dated strategy.

I found the BCD ETF interesting, although it is still not widely used: it has an AUM of only $252.92M.

However, it meets my research criteria. It is a K-1 free ETF with a longer-dated strategy, an expense ratio of 0.30%, a yield of 3.37%, and a 5Y yield growth rate of 24.28%.

I know that longer-dated strategy ETFs are less popular among commodity investors, but let me explain my choice: typically, this strategy is more effective in handling contango, which helps maintain lower volatility. And I know that this means a higher expense ratio and (typically) lower performance, but BCD appears to offer a reasonable trade-off.

It has maintained low volatility (9.56) and delivered strong performance compared to standard rolling ETFs. When comparing BCD to its standard rolling counterpart, BCI, the performance difference is striking—almost 30%. This is despite the higher cost and the (theoretically) lower ability to capture short-term market spikes in commodities.

Stagflation risk: manage it with BCD ETF

In recent days, a new concern has been developing in the stock market: this fear is called stagflation. I know, it's being mentioned in many analyses, and it sounds a bit excessive to me as well. However, the markets appear to be adopting this scenario - here's an interesting piece of evidence:

A decisive shift in trend compared to only a month ago

There are three notable developments in the market today:

  • A sudden shift in Treasury yields (-).
  • A surge in gold (commodities) prices (+).
  • A gradual depreciation of the US dollar (-).

Only one month ago:

  • Yields were rising (+).
  • Gold (commodities) was appreciating (+).
  • The dollar was reaching its highest levels of the year (+).
Markets End 2024 2025
Treasury - +
Commodities + +
Dollar + -

What's different now?

The market perspective has changed:

The economic data from January painted a picture of strong resilience in the US market. The inflation rate increased by 0.5%, reaching 3.1% YoY, while the unemployment rate has decreased to 4%. The writing was on the wall: the US economy was aligning with GDP growth expectations (over 2.1%), and the Fed was not going to cut rates.

However, the market opinion has changed again. This is probably driven by the drop in consumer confidence reported by the Conference Board, a strong dissonance compared to expectations.

This led to a decline in yields and a depreciation of the dollar, almost pricing in an incoming recession. It's no coincidence that recession expectations for the next year have risen to a nine-month high.

So, what's next?

I believe the consumer confidence data should not be underestimated. That said, as usual, I prefer to base my analysis on the data. The fact that commodity prices have maintained their trend while Treasuries and DXY have shifted is a strong bullish indicator. This suggests that (following the market movement) despite concerns about an economic slowdown, inflation expectations remain bullish. Put differently, I think that rather than a recession risk, a "stagflation" risk has come into play. This is bringing a lot of uncertainty to the markets, and "uncertainty" is typically an investor's greatest fear.

Personally, I looked for a solution in the only market that has shown strength (+) in both scenarios priced in by the market: commodities.

But why, specifically, could BCD serve as a balanced solution between these two scenarios?

Why BCD?

BCD invests in fixed income and commodities, tracking the performance of the Bloomberg Commodity Index 3-Month Forward Total Return.

I tried to reclassify the exposures of its benchmark to achieve this redistribution:

The outcome is a holding breakdown that I consider especially intriguing in light of the historical context ahead.

Indeed, this allocation creates a compelling balance: combining a yield of 3% with the commodities sector, historically strong during periods of high inflation, while mitigating potential declines that Treasuries might experience in a stagflation scenario.

Risk

As always, there is a risk to consider. The BCD ETF is illiquid, with an AUM of only $252.92M. This can lead to:

  1. Deviation from the NAV: compared to other ETFs, such as the well-known PDBC, it could face issues related to index tracking.
  2. A higher bid-ask spread than other ETFs: this could affect both purchase and sale transactions.

Therefore, when evaluating the cost associated with this ETF, it is prudent to also consider this aspect.

Conclusion

BCD ETF could potentially capture the benefits of high interest rates, take advantage of positive inflation, and possibly hedge against stagflation risks.

This is due to its well-balanced exposure and longer-dated strategy, which appears to maintain a controlled level of volatility without sacrificing returns.
